name: Computed Fields vs Transclusions: Gap Analysis

content.description:
**Status:** Assessment (March 2026)

An analysis of what [Computed Fields](item://c0c0c0c0-0074-0000-0000-000000000000) and [Structured Notes](item://c0c0c0c0-0073-0000-0000-000000000000) provide compared to the existing [Transclusion](item://c0c0c0c0-0035-0000-0000-000000000000) system, and what gaps must be closed before transclusions could be removed.

## What Transclusions Provide Today

The transclusion system (`![](item://...)` in [hobson-markdown](item://a1b2c3d4-5e6f-7a8b-9c0d-e1f2a3b4c5d6)) provides:

| Feature | Syntax |
|---------|--------|
| Full item rendering | `![](item://id)` — renders entire item via `renderItem()` |
| Field extraction | `![](item://id#description)` — pulls a specific field |
| Region extraction | `![](item://id#code?region=name)` — named `[BEGIN:]/[END:]` regions |
| Line range | `![](item://id#code?lines=10-20)` |
| Symbol extraction | `![](item://id?symbol=fnName)` — resolves via `_symbols` metadata |
| Render mode override | `?render=markdown` or `?render=code` |
| Functional transclusion | `![](item://id?call=fn)` — calls module export, returns DOM or string |
| Query blocks | ` ```query ``` ` — arbitrary JS with `api`, `transclude()`, `transcludeEach()` |
| Collapsible chrome | `<details>` box with "Transcluded from" header and navigation link |
| Editor gesture | "Insert Link/Transclusion" toolbar button with item search modal |

## What Computed Fields Cover

The computed fields system (`content.computed` + `{{field-name}}`) covers:

| Feature | Equivalent |
|---------|------------|
| Field transclusion | `(get-field @other :description)` |
| Functional transclusion | `(invoke (require "module") "fn" api params)` |
| Computed values | Any Hob expression — arithmetic, aggregation, etc. |
| Cross-item reactivity | Automatic re-render when referenced items change |
| Cycle detection | `_computingFields` Set prevents infinite recursion |
| Error sentinels | Graceful `ERR` display with tooltip, error items via `captureError` |
| Provenance links | AST walk extracts `@`-referenced items in field summary |
| Inline rendering | `{{field-name}}` in markdown body with dashed underline, clickable |

Computed fields are stronger than transclusions in reactivity, error handling, and extensibility to non-transclusion use cases (spreadsheet calculations, aggregations). But they don't cover everything.

## Gaps

### 1. Full item rendering — no equivalent

Transclusion renders an *entire item* via `renderItem()` inside a `<details>` box — the full view, not just a single field. Computed fields can only return a single field value. There's no `{{}}` equivalent for "embed this item's complete rendered view here."

**Possible fix:** A Hob function like `(render-item @other-item)` that returns a DOM element. The functional transclusion path already supports DOM returns, so this may be straightforward.

### 2. Region extraction (`?region=name`) — no equivalent

Transclusions can pull a named `[BEGIN:name]/[END:name]` region from a code field. `get-field` returns the entire field value.

**Possible fix:** A stdlib function `(extract-region (get-field @item :code) "regionName")`.

### 3. Line range extraction (`?lines=10-20`) — no equivalent

Extracting a slice of a multiline string.

**Possible fix:** `(slice-lines (get-field @item :code) 10 20)`.

### 4. Symbol extraction (`?symbol=fnName`) — no equivalent

Transclusions resolve symbols via `_symbols` metadata to extract function/class definitions. No Hob function exposes this.

**Possible fix:** `(extract-symbol @item "fnName")` that reads `_symbols` and applies the line range.

### 5. Query blocks — no equivalent

` ```query ``` ` blocks execute arbitrary JS with full `api` access and return DOM or markdown. Computed fields use Hob and are per-field, not inline blocks. The `transclude()` and `transcludeEach()` helpers for attribution chrome have no Hob equivalents.

This is the biggest gap. Query blocks enable dynamic lists, filtered views, and aggregation dashboards with flexible output formatting. Computed fields can do *some* of this via `(reduce + 0 (map ...))`, but can't easily produce complex multi-item markdown with attribution chrome.

This may need to remain as a separate mechanism, or evolve into a Hob equivalent.

### 6. Collapsible chrome with provenance — partially covered

Transclusions render in collapsible `<details>` boxes with "Transcluded from: [Name] ↗" headers. Computed field `{{refs}}` render inline with dashed underlines and click-to-scroll — no visual boxing, no collapse, no "from" attribution at the reference site. Provenance is only shown in the field summary section at the bottom.

This is a deliberate design choice (inline vs. block), but for cases where the user wants to embed a paragraph from another note with visible attribution, the old chrome is more appropriate.

### 7. Editor gesture for computed transclusions — not implemented

The old editor has an "Insert Link/Transclusion" button that opens a search modal and inserts `![name](item://id)` at the cursor. The [Structured Notes](item://c0c0c0c0-0073-0000-0000-000000000000) doc describes a gesture that creates both the `content.computed` entry and the `{{name}}` reference at the cursor, but this isn't built yet.

Without this, creating a computed-field transclusion requires: (1) switch to edit mode, (2) add a computed field in the computed-fields section, (3) write the formula manually, (4) go back to the body and type `{{name}}`. Four steps vs. one click.

### 8. Render mode override — no equivalent

`?render=code` forces code-block rendering of a description field. Computed fields return raw values — the rendering is always inline text.

## UX Observations

### Formula authoring is too raw

Creating a computed field requires writing Hob AST in the structural editor. For the common case of "pull field X from item Y", this is `(get-field @item-id :field-name)` — reasonable for developers, but intimidating for note-taking. Consider:
- A "quick transclusion" builder: pick item, pick field, auto-generate the formula
- Template formulas for common patterns (transclusion, sum, count)

### `{{field-name}}` conflates identity with display

The field name is both the programmatic key in `content.computed` and the human-visible reference in the body. Renaming a computed field breaks every `{{old-name}}` in the body.

Consider auto-renaming `{{refs}}` in the body when a computed field is renamed in the editor.

### No preview of computed values in the editor

When editing, you see raw `{{tokens}}`, not resolved values. A live preview pane or inline resolution would help.

### Field summary feels like a debugging tool

The field summary at the bottom showing formulas + resolved values + provenance is powerful but feels more like a developer inspector. Consider:
- Collapsed by default for notes with few fields
- A "compact" mode showing only values, not formulas
- Provenance links inline near the `{{ref}}` rather than only at the bottom

### Error UX is minimal

A red "ERR" span with a tooltip is functional but not discoverable — users won't always hover. Consider:
- An error count badge in the field summary header
- Clicking ERR navigates to the error item or opens the formula editor

### No reverse dependency lookup

Provenance shows "this formula references items A, B, C" (forward refs), but there's no reverse lookup — "what items have formulas that reference *this* item?" The "used by" direction would be valuable for understanding impact before editing.

## Verdict: Can Transclusions Be Removed?

**Not yet.** Five functional gaps (full item rendering, region/line/symbol extraction, query blocks) and one critical UX gap (editor gesture) remain.

Computed fields handle the common case of "pull a field value from another item" well, with better reactivity and error handling. But transclusions serve broader use cases — especially code documentation with region/symbol extraction and query-driven dynamic content.

## Recommended Path

1. **Keep both systems** — they're orthogonal and don't conflict
2. **Build the editor gesture** for creating computed transclusions (highest UX impact)
3. **Add stdlib functions** for region/line/symbol extraction (closes the code-doc gap)
4. **Evaluate query blocks separately** — these may evolve into a Hob equivalent or remain as JS escape hatches
5. **Author real notes** using computed fields to discover which gaps matter in practice

## References

- [Structured Notes](item://c0c0c0c0-0073-0000-0000-000000000000) — Computed fields merged into the note type
- [Reactive Computed Fields](item://c0c0c0c0-0074-0000-0000-000000000000) — The underlying mechanism
- [Transclusion](item://c0c0c0c0-0035-0000-0000-000000000000) — The existing system
- [hobson-markdown](item://a1b2c3d4-5e6f-7a8b-9c0d-e1f2a3b4c5d6) — Implements transclusion rendering
